facts.claim: HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple requests over a single TCP connection, fixing HTTP-transaction-level head-of-line blocking from HTTP/1.x but introducing TCP-level head-of-line blocking
HTTP/3 with QUIC achieves 12.4% better TTFB than HTTP/2: 176ms vs 201ms average at Cloudflare
QUIC combines transport and cryptographic handshakes, reducing connection setup from 2-3 RTTs to 0-1 RTTs for repeat connections
Traditional TCP with TLS 1.2 requires 3 RTT of handshake overhead (~240ms on 80ms RTT mobile networks); TLS 1.3 reduces to 2 RTT (1 TCP + 1 TLS), saving 80ms
Session resumption costs 50% less than full TLS handshake because it requires only 1 RTT vs 2, and avoids expensive asymmetric cryptography
Full TLS handshakes consume 5 to 10x more CPU than resumed sessions due to expensive asymmetric cryptography (ECDHE key generation, signature verification)
Session resumption rates above 80% mean only 20% pay full handshake cost, saving hundreds of CPU cores at millions of requests per second
TLS 1.3 0-RTT session resumption enables instant reconnection for repeat visitors by reusing pre-shared keys without additional handshake
Layer 4 load balancing makes routing decisions based on IPs and TCP/UDP ports only; Layer 7 makes decisions based on HTTP headers, URL, cookies, message content
Layer 7 load balancers maintain two TCP connections (client + server) and reassemble packets; Layer 4 processes packet-by-packet without application visibility
Cache-Control 'immutable' directive avoids unnecessary conditional requests for static resources when combined with cache-busting patterns and long max-age
For static content, Google recommends cache duration longer than 6 months or even 1 year for immutable resources with version hashes in URLs
For HTML and API responses, no-cache ensures revalidation on every request; private cache flag prevents CDN caching of personalized content
ETag and Last-Modified headers enable efficient cache revalidation without re-downloading full content if unchanged
Time-to-First-Byte (TTFB) good threshold is under 800ms; 800-1800ms needs improvement; over 1800ms is poor per Google 2025 guidance
Sites with poor LCP spend average of 2.27 seconds on TTFB alone, nearly exhausting entire 2.5s LCP threshold before rendering begins
Enabling server-level caching with Varnish or Nginx FastCGI can drop TTFB from 400ms to under 20ms for cached pages
Dynamic content generation is the most common culprit for high TTFB; slow database queries and PHP execution dominate
HTTP/2 reduces active connections carrying single transactions from 74% (HTTP/1) to 25% via persistent connection multiplexing
HTTP/2 connection reuse eliminates expensive TCP handshake and TLS negotiation costs, reducing total protocol overhead
HTTP/3 on 4G networks with 15% packet loss improves page load times by 55% compared to HTTP/2
HTTP/3 shows 52% faster download times on unstable mobile networks with high latency and packet loss vs HTTP/2
With HTTP/2, lost packet blocks all files until retransmission (head-of-line blocking); HTTP/3 streams independently so other resources continue
Google's QUIC deployment reduced search result load time by 8% on desktop, 3.6% on mobile; up to 16% faster for slowest 1% of users
YouTube video on QUIC shows up to 20% less stalling in countries like India due to improved loss recovery
69% of HTTP/3 connections reached 5 Mbps+ throughput vs 56% for HTTP/2 (Netflix minimum threshold for Full HD streaming)
TLS session IDs stored on server-side; TLS session tickets stored on client-side, removing server cache burden entirely
Session resumption key derivation costs 1/10 of full key exchange computation due to avoiding asymmetric cryptography
TLS session IDs limited by server memory for concurrent connections; TLS 1.3 uses session tickets exclusively, allowing multiple tickets per connection
Waterfall chart phases: DNS Lookup, TCP Connect (1 RTT), TLS Handshake, Waiting/TTFB, Content Download; typical total 100-500ms
Real-world waterfall example: DNS 0.755ms, TCP 35.484ms, TLS Handshake 36.363ms for single request
HTTPS connection requires 1 RTT for TCP + 1-2 RTTs for TLS before first request sends; 100ms RTT = 200-300ms pure handshake latency
HTTP/2 server push can harm performance if used on resources that vary by request; clogging the network when combined with multiplexing
HTTP/2 connection coalescing allows reusing one connection for multiple domains if IP addresses match and TLS cert includes all SANs/wildcard
HTTP/2 ORIGIN Frame extension enables connection coalescing without managing identical IP addresses across domains
BBR is latency-based congestion control using packet-pacing, insensitive to random loss; CUBIC uses packet loss as congestion indicator
CUBIC is default QUIC congestion control; BBR variants being advanced in quiche; Google, Amazon, Akamai, Meta, Cloudflare use BBRv1
BBRv2 offers robust throughput in shallow buffers with HyStart++ alleviating startup losses; more sensitive to loss than BBRv1
Head-of-line blocking at TCP level remains a design flaw in HTTP/2; packet-level blocking of TCP stream blocks all multiplexed transactions
Mobile and wireless networks particularly suffer from HTTP/2 head-of-line blocking due to higher packet loss rates

examples.metric: Enabling HTTP/3 reduced first byte from 201ms (HTTP/2) to 176ms average
5 Mbps minimum for Full HD; 69% HTTP/3 connections vs 56% HTTP/2 reached this threshold
QUIC reduced search load time 8% desktop, 3.6% mobile; 16% for slowest 1% of users
Up to 20% less video stalling on QUIC in high-loss markets like India
HTTP/3 improved page load 55% vs HTTP/2; 52% faster downloads on unstable networks
80%+ resumption rate saves hundreds of CPU cores by avoiding asymmetric crypto
Cached page TTFB dropped from 400ms to under 20ms
Average 2.27 seconds spent on TTFB alone (Google 2025 Web Almanac)
74% of active HTTP/1 connections carry single transaction; HTTP/2 drops to 25%
All major CDNs deployed HTTP/3 across global networks by 2024

analogies.analogy: Like a single-lane highway: even though cars can coordinate internally (multiplexing), if one car hits a pothole and stops, all cars behind it must wait (TCP-level blocking).
Unlike HTTP/2's single highway, HTTP/3 is like multiple parallel lanes: if one image loses a packet, CSS and JavaScript files continue on their lanes unblocked.
Building a house (full handshake) takes months; furnishing it takes days. Session resumption is like keeping furniture in storage—retrieve what you need, skip construction.
Session ID = server memorizing every customer's preferences (memory burden); Session Ticket = customer carries their preferences in wallet (zero server burden).
Layer 4 = postal service routing by zip code only; Layer 7 = mail sorter reading envelope contents and address to determine best recipient department.
Like a time capsule: once sealed with a version number, it never changes, so you can keep it forever without checking inside.
Like a VIP entering a club: they skip the line (0-RTT data) using a membership card (pre-shared key) instead of going through ID check (full handshake).
CUBIC = driver watching brake lights ahead and hitting brakes when traffic stops (loss-based); BBR = driver constantly checking road conditions and adjusting speed smoothly (latency-based).

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP/2 fixes all head-of-line blocking problems
common_misconceptions.reality: HTTP/2 fixes application-layer HOL blocking but introduces TCP-level HOL: any lost/delayed TCP packet blocks all multiplexed streams simultaneously

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP/2 is always faster than HTTP/1.1
common_misconceptions.reality: HTTP/2 provides benefits for most workloads, but performance depends on network conditions; some high-latency scenarios or specific implementations may show minimal gains

common_misconceptions.misconception: More TCP connections always means better HTTP/2 performance
common_misconceptions.reality: HTTP/2 is designed around single persistent connection per origin; multiple connections defeat multiplexing benefits and waste resources

common_misconceptions.misconception: Server push solves all HTTP/2 performance problems
common_misconceptions.reality: Server push can harm performance if resources vary by request, create network congestion, or push resources browsers already cached
TLS 1.3 reduces handshake to 1 RTT (vs 2 in TLS 1.2), but TCP connection still requires 1 RTT; QUIC combines both for 1-RTT total

common_misconceptions.misconception: Session IDs are always better for session resumption
common_misconceptions.reality: Session IDs require server-side storage (memory scalability issue); session tickets store state on client side, eliminating server burden at scale

common_misconceptions.misconception: 0-RTT is always safe for any request type
common_misconceptions.reality: 0-RTT lacks forward secrecy and allows replay attacks; must only be used for idempotent operations (GET, not POST/mutations)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Layer 4 load balancing is always faster than Layer 7
common_misconceptions.reality: Layer 4 is faster for simple routing, but Layer 7 enables smarter decisions (session affinity, content-based routing) with acceptable latency for most workloads
ETag and Last-Modified headers required for revalidation; max-age alone doesn't prevent stale cache serving if network communication fails

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP/2 connection coalescing works automatically across all domains
common_misconceptions.reality: Requires explicit IP overlap + TLS cert with SANs/wildcard for both domains; different browsers implement differently; ORIGIN frames offer better alternative

common_misconceptions.misconception: BBR is universally better than CUBIC
common_misconceptions.reality: BBR is latency-optimized but less tested in production; CUBIC is default for QUIC; trade-offs vary by network (shallow buffers, congestion patterns)

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP/3 benefits only matter for mobile users
common_misconceptions.reality: HTTP/3 advantages scale across all networks, especially repeat visitors (0-RTT) and high-loss scenarios; benefits vary but apply to desktop and datacenter scenarios too

common_misconceptions.misconception: TTFB is a Core Web Vital that directly impacts ranking
common_misconceptions.reality: TTFB is diagnostic metric, not CWV, but it directly impacts LCP/FCP which are CWVs; sites with poor TTFB usually have poor LCP

common_misconceptions.misconception: DNS lookup is always the bottleneck in HTTPS connections
common_misconceptions.reality: DNS is often negligible (cached); TCP handshake (1 RTT) + TLS handshake (1-2 RTT) dominate; 200-300ms at 100ms RTT is typical

common_misconceptions.misconception: Packet loss has the same impact on HTTP/2 and HTTP/3
common_misconceptions.reality: HTTP/2 shows 55% slower page loads on 4G with 15% packet loss; HTTP/3 independent streams avoid TCP-level HOL blocking
